frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
Suresh: Aajkal zamaana theek na hai, chhoriyan ne samajh ke chalna chahiye. Raat-biraat akeli ghoomega, idhar-udhar ke ladkon ke saath dikhegi, phir kuchh ulta-seedha ho jaye to rote sab hain.
Brother: Par galti to ladkon ki honi chahiye.
Suresh: Arre wo to theek se, par duniya ek din mein badlegi na. Apni izzat aur suraksha ka dhyan pehle ghar ki chhori ne hi rakhna padega.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women's safety as their own responsibility and normalizes male misbehavior as an unchangeable fact. It uses protective language about 'izzat' and 'suraksha' while advising restrictions on women's movement and company instead of focusing on men's accountability.
original_frame: Other Man's Ownership Over Woman's Own Consent.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. A Jat farmer in a village in Haryana. Your 20 year old daughter Sunita came back from the nearby town where she goes to college and you found out from a neighbour that she has been seen talking to a boy from a lower caste near the college gate. You have not spoken to Sunita yet. You are sitting with your brother and two male cousins in the courtyard in the evening. You are telling them what you heard and what you think needs to be done before this goes further.
